paper_title: The surgical management of a patient with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy and cataracts.
raw_limitation_text:
[CONCLUSION]
Corneal transplantation can nowadays offer the patient not only a chance for vision but also a very good recovery of visual acuity. This case showed, with ample documentation, the difference in duration of the recovery and the comparison of refractive error between penetrating keratoplasty and DMEK. While the visual recovery in both eyes at the one-year mark was very good, this case provided a unique opportunity to observe the real-world advantages and limits of penetrating keratoplasty and DMEK, as well as an opportunity to observe the challenges in the visual recovery process.

Conflict of Interest Statement

The authors state no conflict of interest.

Informed Consent and Human and Animal Rights Statement

Informed consent has been obtained from all individuals included in this study.

Authorization for the use of human subjects

Ethical approval: The research related to human use complies with all the relevant national regulations, and institutional policies, as per the tenets of the Helsinki Declaration, and has been approved by the review board of Clinical Hospital for Ophthalmological Emergencies Bucharest, Bucharest, Romania.

limitations: This case provided a unique opportunity to observe the real-world advantages and limits of penetrating keratoplasty and DMEK.
research_gaps: The study highlights the challenges in the visual recovery process that need further exploration.
future_work: Future research could focus on the long-term outcomes and complications associated with both penetrating keratoplasty and DMEK.
methodological_weaknesses: The study is based on a single case, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
